absolute.brief_justification: Response covers all four dimensions (profitability, cash flow, capital structure/allocation, risks) with mostly accurate figures including FY25 revenue ($281.7B/+15%), operating income ($128.5B/+17%), Microsoft Cloud GM at 69%, operating cash flow ($136.2B/+$17.6B), capex ($64.5B), and the $60B repurchase authorization. However, it misattributes the Q2 FY26 68% figure to Microsoft Cloud GM when it was actually company-wide GM (Microsoft Cloud was 67%). Engagement with anchor [f] is weak — references 'Risk Factors' generically without quoting the specific Item 1A language about cloud/AI infrastructure costs reducing operating margins. Misses the multi-year capex trajectory ($28B→$44.5B→$64.6B) and the $32.1B construction commitment from anchor [d].

The response provides solid segment-level structure with most FY25 and Q2 FY26 figures correctly cited (P&BP $120.8B/+13%, IC $106.3B/+21%/Azure +34%, MPC $54.6B/+7%, gaming -9%, Maya 200/Cobalt 200, 15M Copilot seats). However, two factual errors: (1) 'Devices also declined 15% in FY25' is unsupported — the 10-K only states Devices declined without quantifying 15%, and (2) 'Search and news advertising up 20% ex-TAC in Q2 FY26' misattributes the FY25 figure to Q2 FY26 (actual Q2 FY26 was 10% CC). Also misses three anchors: the FY25 segment reorganization, Microsoft Cloud cross-segment aggregate ($168.9B / $51.5B Q2), and OpenAI partnership specifics (45% RPO, Azure exclusivity, IP rights).

Response follows DECOMPOSE→FRAMEWORKS→SYNTHESIZE structure but execution is compressed and contains factual errors: Search ex-TAC growth attributed to Q2 FY26 as 20% (actual Q2 FY26 figure is 10% with disclosed execution challenges; 20% was FY25 full year), 'Meta LLaMA2' cited as Foundry catalog model (not in target materials), and Q2 FY26 Microsoft Cloud GM stated as 68% (actual is 67%; 68% is company-wide). Engages most anchors but shallowly — completely misses Item 1A risk-factor anchor (h), engages anchor (b) only via 15M seats without DAU/attach-rate context, and doesn't quote the 'demand exceeds supply' language directly. All four economic frameworks named and applied to specific units, synthesis is internally consistent.

Response engages most anchors with accurate figures (revenue $281.7B/15%, op income $128.5B/17%, MSFT Cloud GM 69%, OCF $136.2B, capex $64.6B, $13B repurchases/$24.7B dividends, $60B authorization). However, it includes one unsupported claim about 'FTC opposition to the Activision acquisition' which is not in the target materials (the 10-K only notes the acquisition was completed October 2023). It also fails to explicitly engage Item 1A risk factor language about cloud/AI execution risk reducing operating margins (anchor f). Citations to specific sections are absent; figures are accurate but section-level attribution is weak. Compressed single-paragraph format limits depth.

secondary.brief_justification: The response accurately cites the major financial figures (revenue $281.7B, op income $128.5B, cloud GM 69%, OCF $136.2B, capex $64.6B, $37.7B capital return, $60B buyback authorization) and engages 5 of 6 anchors cleanly. The one miss is anchor MSFT-S-01-f: the Item 1A risk language about cloud/AI services reducing operating margins is never paraphrased or cited by its Item 1A provenance. Additionally, characterizing 'FTC opposition to the Activision acquisition' as a current FY2025 headwind is unsupported in the FY2025 10-K, where the acquisition was already completed and the litigation resolved—the materials do not reference an ongoing FTC challenge in FY25.

absolute.brief_justification: The response delivers a well-organized segment analysis with most figures correctly traced to the 10-K and Q2 FY26 call (P&BP +13%/$120.8B, IC +21%/$106.3B, Azure +34%, M365 Commercial cloud +15%/+17% CC, MPC +7%, 15M Copilot seats, Maya 200/Cobalt 200, ~1GW capacity, $2B Fabric run-rate). However, it asserts 'Devices revenue fell 15% in FY25,' a specific figure not present in the 10-K (which only states 'a decline in Devices' without quantifying). Material anchors missed: the FY25 segment reorganization that consolidated M365 Commercial (anchor a), the Microsoft Cloud cross-segment aggregate at $168.9B / Q2 surpassing $50B (anchor f), and the OpenAI partnership specifics including ~45% RPO concentration, Azure exclusivity, and IP rights (anchor g) — all of which are central to the strategic framing the question invites.

secondary.brief_justification: The response is well-organized and anchors most segment-level figures accurately to the 10-K and Q2 FY26 call (P&BP $120.8B/+13%, IC $106.3B/+21%, Azure +34%, MPC $54.6B/+7%, 15M Copilot seats, Maya 200/Cobalt 200). However, 'Devices revenue fell 15% in FY25' is unsupported — the 10-K only states Windows OEM and Devices rose +3% overall with an unquantified Devices decline. Three key anchors are entirely absent: the Q1 FY2025 segment reorganization moving M365 Commercial into P&BP (anchor a), the $168.9B Microsoft Cloud cross-segment aggregate and the $51.5B quarterly milestone (anchor f), and the OpenAI partnership specifics — API exclusivity to Azure, rights to OpenAI IP, and ~45% of commercial RPO tied to OpenAI (anchor g) — all of which are material to a full strategic analysis.

absolute.brief_justification: The response delivers a well-organized but shallow analysis with all four economic frameworks touched, but only ~4 units (Azure, M365 Commercial, Search, Gaming) receive substantive per-unit economic treatment — Server products, LinkedIn, Dynamics, Windows OEM, and M365 Consumer are listed in decomposition but not analyzed. Factual claims that appear ($625B RPO, $37.5B Q2 capex, 67% MS Cloud GM Q2 FY26, Maya 200 30% TCO, 15M Copilot seats, gaming -9%, 20% search ex-TAC FY25) trace correctly to materials with no fabrications. However, many anchor signals are absent: 45% OpenAI RPO concentration, 4.7M Copilot subs, 1,500+ Foundry customers, Q3 65% gross margin guide, and especially Item 1A risk language are all missed, and the uniformly bullish synthesis does not grapple with management's own articulated downside risks.

Response covers profitability, cash flow, capital allocation, and risks with mostly accurate figures ($281.7B revenue, $136.2B OCF, $64.6B capex, $13B buybacks, $24.7B dividends, 69% Cloud GM, $28.9B IRS NOPA). However, it misses the multi-year capex trajectory ($28B→$44.5B→$64.6B) and the $32.1B construction commitments, and does not explicitly engage Item 1A's risk-factor language about cloud/AI execution risk reducing operating margins (anchor f). Minor inaccuracy: candidate frames memory pricing as affecting margins when materials specifically cite volatility in transactional purchasing. Citations are general rather than section-specific.

secondary.brief_justification: The response is well-organized and accurately deploys key figures ($281.7B revenue, $128.5B operating income, $136.2B OCF, $64.6B capex, $94.6B cash, $13B buybacks, $24.7B dividends, $60B authorization, 69% cloud GM, $28.9B IRS exposure), engaging five of six evidentiary anchors cleanly. The one gap is [MSFT-S-01-f]: the AI-execution risk is never explicitly attributed to Item 1A or paraphrased with the specific 'reducing operating margins' language, rendering it a generic AI-risk reference. A minor grounding issue is the description of FY2025 OCF growth as driven by 'cloud billings and collections,' which is Q2 FY2026 earnings-call language; the 10-K specifically attributes it to 'cash received from customers.' The response also supplements FY2025 data with Q2 FY2026 capex figures without clearly flagging the temporal shift, slightly blurring the scope of the FY2025 assessment.

absolute.brief_justification: All factual claims (Azure +34%/+39% CC, P&BP +13%/+16% CC, MPC +7%/-3%, Xbox hardware -25%, gaming -9% CC, 15M Copilot seats, ~1GW capacity, Maya 200) are accurately sourced from the target materials. However, the response misses several material anchors: it does not discuss the FY25 segment reorganization (anchor a) that consolidated M365 Commercial into P&BP, omits the Microsoft Cloud cross-segment aggregate ($168.9B/+23%, $51.5B Q2; anchor f), and never engages the OpenAI partnership structure including the ~45% RPO concentration disclosed on the Q2 FY26 call (anchor g) — all central to a strategic analysis. Within the segments covered, only one signal per anchor surfaces (e.g., no IC operating income, no LinkedIn/Dynamics specifics, no Server +23%), so depth is shallow.

The response applies all four frameworks and grounds most key figures (Azure 39% CC, $37.5B Q2 capex, 15M Copilot seats, 4.7M GitHub Copilot, $625B RPO with 45% OpenAI, MS Cloud 69% GM, Maya 200/Cobalt 200), but the decomposition is superficial — only Azure, M365 Copilot, and GitHub get distinct per-unit economic treatment, while LinkedIn, Dynamics, Windows OEM, Gaming, Server products are merely named. Misses anchor [h] (Item 1A ROIC/execution risk language) entirely, and [e] substitution is generic without per-unit differentiation or Item 1A attribution; 'Meta' as a Foundry partner is not supported in the target materials (Anthropic, OpenAI, Cohere are mentioned). Synthesis is internally consistent but compressed.

Response engages 5 of 6 anchors with mostly accurate figures across profitability, cash flow, capital structure, and capital allocation. However, contains a factual error: Q2 FY26 Microsoft Cloud gross margin was 67% per the transcript, not 68% (68% was total company GM). Also, the 71% prior-year Microsoft Cloud GM is not explicitly stated in the cited materials. Misses direct engagement with the specific Item 1A risk factor language about cloud/AI services 'reducing operating margins,' instead invoking supply constraints. Structure is clear and citations resolve to real sections.

pairwise.brief_justification: Response B engages more anchor signals (YoY change in cash flow with customer-billing driver, $20.1B capex increase, $60B repurchase authorization, partial Item 1A Risk Factors engagement on supply constraints), while Response A misses YoY drivers and never engages Item 1A. Response A also commits a more material factual error by listing the $75.4B Activision Blizzard acquisition under FY2025 capital allocation when it closed October 2023 (FY2024 per Note 7). Response B has a minor inaccuracy (Q2 FY26 Microsoft Cloud GM was 67%, not 68%) but is otherwise tighter, better-organized, and more thoroughly grounded across profitability, cash flow, capital structure, capital allocation, and risks.

absolute.brief_justification: The response provides a competent segment-level analysis with mostly accurate figures (PBP +13%/$120.8B, IC +21%/$106.3B, Azure +34%, Maya 200 30% TCO, 15M Copilot seats, etc.). However, it conflates the FY25 annual Xbox hardware -25% figure with Q2 FY26 timeframe ('By Q2 FY26...Xbox hardware plummeted 25%'), a temporal misattribution. Three material anchors are missed: the FY25 segment reorganization (M365 commercial consolidation), the cross-segment Microsoft Cloud aggregate ($168.9B / $50B+ quarterly milestone), and the OpenAI partnership specifics (API exclusivity to Azure, ~45% of commercial RPO). These gaps limit strategic depth despite solid execution on what is included.

The response correctly executes DECOMPOSE → APPLY → SYNTHESIZE with all four frameworks applied to Azure and consistent unit-level reasoning, but evidentiary engagement is shallow — it misses the $625B RPO / 45% OpenAI concentration disclosure entirely, ignores Item 1A risk factor language, omits specific capex figures ($64.6B FY25, $37.5B Q2, $32.1B committed), and provides only thin treatment of Foundry's multi-model catalog. Includes a fabrication (Meta listed as a Foundry model — not in target materials) and a margin conflation (Q2 FY26 Microsoft Cloud gross margin was 67%, not 68% as stated). Framework discipline is solid but quantitative depth and anchor coverage are limited for a 5+ year thesis question.

secondary.brief_justification: The response applies all four frameworks, cites correct figures where it goes (34%/39% Azure growth, 15M Copilot seats, 69%/68% cloud margins, Maya 200 30% TCO, Gaming -9%), and is internally consistent in synthesis. However, it completely omits the $625B commercial RPO and its 45% OpenAI concentration profile (anchor [a] — one of the most material demand-visibility disclosures), never cites the Item 1A risk language on open-source AI or on cloud/AI execution risk (anchors [e] and [h]), and does not engage Azure AI Foundry / multi-model specifics with required signals (anchor [c]). Only 4 of 8 anchors are substantively engaged. LinkedIn and Dynamics 365 are named in the decompose but receive no per-unit economic analysis. The mention of 'Meta' as a Foundry model lacks support in the target materials. These gaps prevent this from rising above a solid-but-incomplete effort.

absolute.brief_justification: Response covers the four required dimensions (profitability, cash flow, capital structure/allocation, risks) with mostly accurate top-line figures, but engagement is shallow. It heavily leans on Q2 FY26 data (e.g., $37.5B capex, 68% GM, $12.7B return) when the question asks about FY2025 — missing key FY25 anchors like $64.6B capex, $32.1B construction commitment, and the FY25 Microsoft Cloud GM of 69%. The $37.2B shareholder return aggregate is slightly off (declared total is $37.7B) and lacks the disaggregation ($13B repurchases / $24.7B dividends) the anchor calls for. No Item 1A risk factor is quoted or attributed to management. Citations are absent — claims are stated without section references.

The response provides a coherent dual-engine framework with reasonable segment characterization, but misses three of seven anchors: the FY25 segment reorganization (a), the Microsoft Cloud cross-segment aggregate of $168.9B / $51.5B Q2 milestone (f), and OpenAI partnership specifics like Azure exclusivity / IP rights / ~45% RPO share (g). Contains factual errors: Search and news ex-TAC grew 10% in CC in Q2 FY26 (the 20% figure is FY25), and the 25% Xbox hardware drop is a FY25 figure being framed as Q2 FY26 context. Otherwise, headline segment figures and Q2 Copilot/silicon callouts are accurate.

Response covers the required structure with all four frameworks applied to multiple units, but contains a factual error attributing FY25's full-year search ex-TAC growth of 20% to Q2 FY26 (actual Q2 FY26 figure was 10% CC, 'slightly below expectations'). Engagement with anchors is mostly minimal — RPO/OpenAI share and CapEx $37.5B figures land, but the response misses Item 1A risk factor language entirely (anchor h), omits Copilot adoption specifics like 4.7M subscribers or attach-rate framing, and lacks specific gross margin trajectory figures (FY25 69% / Q3 FY26 guide 65%). Synthesis is internally consistent but reasoning is comparatively thin on quantitative substantiation.

secondary.brief_justification: The response covers the mandatory three-part structure, correctly cites the $625B RPO figure (with 45% OpenAI), 15M paid M365 Copilot seats, and the Q2 FY26 CapEx of $37.5B, and applies all four economic frameworks to at least one unit each. However, it contains a material factual error — attributing the FY25 full-year search ex-TAC growth of 20% to Q2 FY26 (the actual Q2 FY26 figure was 10% CC, 'slightly below expectations') — and misclassifies GitHub into the Productivity and Business Processes segment when it belongs to Intelligent Cloud. Three anchors go unengaged: the Item 1A language on execution and ROIC risk, the specific Foundry/multi-model catalog signals (GPT-5 + Claude 4.5, 1,500+ dual-model customers, Agent 365), and the open-source substitution risk cited in Item 1A and the Azure competition description, all of which are material to a rigorous AI-diffusion thesis.

absolute.brief_justification: Response covers all required dimensions (profitability, cash flow, capital structure/allocation, risks) with mostly accurate figures, but has notable gaps: it omits the FY25 capex figure ($64.6B) and multi-year trajectory, instead substituting Q2 FY26's $37.5B; misses the $32.1B construction commitment; doesn't engage with the Item 1A cloud/AI execution risk language; and skips revenue ($281.7B/+15%) and segment-wide growth framing. The '71% in FY24' Microsoft Cloud GM figure is not directly stated in the materials (only the decrease 'to 69%' is given). Capital allocation, IRS dispute, and IDPC details are well-grounded.

pairwise.brief_justification: Response A engages more anchors with concrete figures (revenue $281.7B/+15%, op income +17%, $136.2B operating cash flow with $17.6B YoY increase, $64.6B capex, full capital return triplet) and cites specific 10-K locations (Note 11 for IRS NOPAs, Note 10 for debt, Item 1A). Response B drops the FY25 revenue figure entirely, omits the $17.6B operating cash flow increase and the $64.6B FY25 capex figure (substituting Q2 FY26 data), and includes a questionable 'down from 71% in FY24' Microsoft Cloud gross margin claim that is not stated in the provided 10-K. A is also more scope-adherent to the FY2025 question, while B over-relies on Q2 FY26 earnings call data.

absolute.brief_justification: Response is well-grounded — every numeric claim (P&BP $120.8B/+13%, IC $106.3B/+21%/Azure +34%, MPC $54.6B/+7%, 15M Copilot seats, Maya 200/Cobalt 200, 1GW capacity, Gaming -9%) traces accurately to the 10-K and Q2 FY26 transcript. However, breadth is limited: response engages anchors b, c, d, and partially e, but misses the FY25 segment reorganization (anchor a) — a material strategic disclosure — the Microsoft Cloud cross-segment framing ($168.9B / $51.5B Q2; anchor f), and the OpenAI partnership structure / ~45% RPO concentration (anchor g). Strategic analysis is competent but compact, lacking depth on management priorities beyond surface-level segment commentary.

pairwise.brief_justification: Response B engages anchor [e] more strongly by explicitly naming Maya 200, Cobalt 200, and the 'token factory' framing from Nadella's prepared remarks, and surfaces gross-margin compression in Intelligent Cloud (anchor [c]). Response B also captures MPC drivers more accurately, noting both Activision lifting Gaming AND Search +20% ex-TAC, whereas Response A overstates MPC growth as 'primarily due to' Activision and omits the Search driver. Response A wins on clarity (cleaner segment headers) and citation formatting (more explicit tags), and uniquely engages anchor [f] by referencing the $50B Microsoft Cloud quarterly milestone, but B's stronger anchor coverage on e/c/d carries the day. Both responses miss anchors [a] (segment reorganization) and [g] (OpenAI partnership specifics).

secondary.brief_justification: Every numeric claim in the response is traceable to source materials (P&BP $120.8B +13%, IC $106.3B +21%, Azure +34%, MPC $54.6B +7%, 15M Copilot seats, gaming -9% CC, nearly 1 GW added), and the three-layer framing from Nadella's Q2 FY26 remarks is captured. However, the response misses three material anchors: the Q1 FY2025 segment reorganization (which signals management's strategic prioritization of unified M365 Commercial cloud), the Microsoft Cloud cross-segment aggregate ($168.9B FY25, $51.5B quarterly milestone), and the OpenAI partnership structure (API exclusivity to Azure, IP rights, ~45% of commercial RPO concentration) — the last of which is a pivotal strategic and concentration-risk disclosure for any segment-level analysis. These omissions leave the strategic narrative incomplete for a senior research standard.

absolute.brief_justification: Response follows the decompose-apply-synthesize structure and applies all four frameworks at a basic level, but the decomposition is very shallow — only Azure and M365 Commercial Cloud receive genuine per-unit economic observations, while LinkedIn, Dynamics 365, Server products, Windows OEM, Gaming, and Search are absent or name-dropped without per-unit analysis. Numeric claims (Azure 34%/39%, $37.5B capex, 69% cloud GM, Maya 200 30% TCO, GitHub Copilot +77% QoQ) are accurate. However, evidentiary breadth is weak: misses the $625B RPO and 45% OpenAI concentration entirely, misses WorkIQ/data-graph framing for M365, misses Item 1A management risk language, and engages Copilot adoption only via the 77% figure without 4.7M, 15M, or attach-rate context.

Response is well-structured and covers all four required dimensions (profitability, cash flow, capital structure/allocation, risks) with accurate figures for revenue ($281.7B/+15%), operating income ($128.5B/+17%), Cloud GM (69%), operating cash flow ($136.2B/+$17.6B), capex ($64.6B), capital return ($13.0B buybacks/$24.7B dividends/$60B authorization), and the IRS NOPA ($28.9B). Minor weakness: the '71% to 69%' Microsoft Cloud GM comparison includes a prior-period figure (71%) not explicitly stated in the target 10-K. Misses anchor [f] — does not cite or paraphrase the Item 1A risk factor language about AI/cloud execution risk reducing operating margins; also omits the $32.1B datacenter construction commitment and multi-year capex trajectory ($28B→$44.5B→$64.6B).

pairwise.brief_justification: Response B engages substantially more anchors with greater specificity: it cites the 15%/17% growth rates (anchor a), the $17.6B YoY operating cash flow increase (anchor c), the full $64.6B capex figure with AI context (anchor d, which A barely touches), and the $60B repurchase authorization (anchor e). Response B is also better organized into thematic sections. Response A is more concise and stays tighter to the 10-K (B reaches into the Q2 FY26 call), but engages fewer anchors at less depth, particularly missing the capex trajectory entirely. B's claim of '71% to 69%' Microsoft Cloud GM declination is a minor concern (the 10-K only explicitly states the 69% endpoint), but does not outweigh its broader and more specific anchor engagement; neither response strongly engages anchor f (Item 1A risk factor language).

absolute.brief_justification: Response correctly engages anchors b, c, d, and e with accurate segment figures (P&BP $120.8B/+13%, IC $106.3B/+21%, MPC $54.6B/+7%, Azure +34% FY25 / +39% Q2 FY26) and the three-layer framework (Maya 200/Cobalt 200, Foundry, 15M Copilot seats). However, it misses three meaningful anchors: the FY25 segment reorganization (a), the Microsoft Cloud cross-segment aggregate of $168.9B / $51.5B Q2 (f), and OpenAI partnership specifics including the ~45% RPO concentration (g). One claim—'Devices revenue fell 15%'—is not directly supported by the 10-K, which only states Windows OEM and Devices grew 3% with an unspecified Devices decline.

secondary.brief_justification: The response is well-organized and accurately cites segment-level figures for all three segments (IC $106.3B +21%, Azure +34%, PBP $120.8B +13%, MPC $54.6B +7%), Q2 FY26 Azure +39%, 15M Copilot seats, Xbox hardware -25%, and correctly attributes the three-layer AI strategy to Nadella's Q2 FY26 call. However, it carries one unsupported specific claim ('Devices revenue fell 15%'—the 10-K notes a Devices decline but gives no specific percentage), and it fails to engage three material anchors: the FY25 segment reorganization/recast (MSFT-S-02-a), the cross-segment Microsoft Cloud metric of $168.9B FY25 and $51.5B quarterly milestone (MSFT-S-02-f), and the strategically critical OpenAI partnership structure including ~45% of commercial RPO and API exclusivity on Azure (MSFT-S-02-g)—a significant omission for a strategic analysis.

absolute.brief_justification: Response is well-grounded with no fabrications and engages 5-6 of 8 anchors (RPO $625B, Copilot adoption metrics, Foundry/Agent 365, $37.5B capex with 'demand exceeds supply' framing, Microsoft Cloud margin trajectory, Maya 200 TCO). However, it falls short of MSFT-S-03's structural mandate: decomposition lists LinkedIn, Dynamics, Gaming, Windows OEM, Server products by name but provides no per-unit economic observation for them; frameworks are combined rather than applied unit-by-unit; and key bear-case anchors are skipped entirely (Item 1A risk language on AI investment ROI, WorkIQ data-graph framing, Copilot attach-rate context at 15M/450M, Q3 FY26 65% gross margin guide). Synthesis is bullish and internally consistent but does not deeply engage tension between margin compression and free-cash-flow expansion claims.

pairwise.brief_justification: Response B engages substantially more evidentiary anchors (commercial RPO of $625B, Foundry multi-model catalog with Anthropic+OpenAI+Cohere, Agent 365 control plane, contracted-useful-life framing for GPU capex, Maya/Cobalt silicon, and the 39% Azure print) while Response A omits RPO entirely, says nothing about OpenAI concentration, and provides only a generic Foundry mention. Both responses miss WorkIQ (anchor d) and Item 1A self-articulated downside language (anchor h), and B contains one factual slip — stating Microsoft Cloud Q2 FY26 gross margin was 68% when it was actually 67% (68% is the company-wide figure) — which is why citation accuracy edges to A; even so, B's deeper synthesis on supply constraints, vertical integration, and substitution dynamics produces a more rigorous and internally consistent thesis.

secondary.brief_justification: The response engages roughly 6 of 8 anchors but critically misses [MSFT-S-03-d] (no mention of WorkIQ or M365 as a stateful-agent data/context layer) and [MSFT-S-03-h] (no engagement with management's own Item 1A articulation of downside risk), making the synthesis one-sidedly bullish without the rigor the rubric demands. Two material factual errors undermine groundedness: Q2 FY26 Microsoft Cloud gross margin is described as 68% when Hood explicitly stated 67% (68% was company-level), and GPU investments are described as 'long-lived' when the transcript explicitly classifies them as short-lived assets (roughly two-thirds of Q2 FY26 capex). The decomposition lists nine sub-units but only Azure, M365 Commercial, and Search receive any per-unit economic reasoning, leaving LinkedIn, Dynamics, Windows, Gaming, Server, and M365 Consumer without substantive framework treatment.
